kD3-DKkiVeA.0: social inequality, or the climate crisis. So are there ways to get unstuck? People often report
kD3-DKkiVeA.1: feeling highly motivated at the start and end of a project, but the middle can feel untethered.
kD3-DKkiVeA.2: Psychologists call this the stuck-in-the-middle effect, and it's thought to be caused by a shift
kD3-DKkiVeA.3: in perspective that happens as you move closer to your goals. When you start working towards
kD3-DKkiVeA.4: something, any progress can feel like a win, as we tend to compare ourselves against where we
kD3-DKkiVeA.5: started. For example, when someone starts training for a marathon, accomplishing even a short jog
kD3-DKkiVeA.6: can feel motivating. But the longer you work on something, the more likely it is that your focus
kD3-DKkiVeA.7: shifts to your end goal. In other words, rather than seeing how far you've come,
kD3-DKkiVeA.8: it's easy to become frustrated by how far you still need to go. A simple way to avoid these
kD3-DKkiVeA.9: motivational sinkholes is to break your pursuits into smaller, more achievable sub-goals. This creates less

kOEDG3j1bjs.3: types of black holes, with stellar and supermassive black holes being the most common. Stellar
kOEDG3j1bjs.4: black holes form when massive stars die and collapse. They're roughly 10 to 20 times the
kOEDG3j1bjs.5: mass of our sun, and scattered throughout the universe. There could be millions of these
kOEDG3j1bjs.6: stellar black holes in the Milky Way alone. Supermassive black holes are giants by comparison,
